source.title: Imagination as a fundamental function of the hippocampus
source.venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B

confidence_rationale: Phil Trans B review by Frank lab; proposes that the fundamental hippocampal function is generating hypothetical experiences and thoughts -- a broader claim than memory retrieval. Directly addresses the question of hippocampus as option generator vs. archive. Good source quality (Phil Trans B themed issue); strong mapping fidelity to ARC-007. Limited primary data specific to behavioural sequence retrieval.
failure_signatures: The imagination framing is under-constrained: firing patterns corresponding to alternatives to actual experience are shown, but the functional role in guiding specific choices is not always established.
The review acknowledges that many of the 'imagination' firing events may be epiphenomenal with respect to behaviour.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal firing patterns regularly correspond to alternatives to actual experience -- including locations not currently occupied, paths not taken, and scenarios with no direct past/future reference -- suggesting the fundamental hippocampal function is generating hypothetical experiences.
mapping.ree_translation: The 'imagination' framing here is the ARC-007 claim stated from the other direction: if hippocampus generates hypothetical trajectories that are not necessarily past memories, it functions as a cue-queryable option space rather than a passive archive. This supports the idea that different PFC cues can retrieve different non-experienced options from the hippocampal store.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The Frank/Kay 2022 framing is explicitly broader than the cue-indexed store model; it does not require that the imagination events are specifically triggered by PFC cues.
mapping.source_context: Themed issue in Phil Trans B on 'Thinking about possibilities'; synthesis of rodent and human hippocampal firing data; Frank lab (UCSF), Kay lab (Columbia).

confidence_components.notes: Conceptually strong; limited on the cue-trigger specificity aspect of the claim.
